policy_statement: NAP target: irrigated infrastructure area with precision technologies promoting efficient water use to reach 10% by 2020 and 50% by 2030.
mechanism_description: Expand irrigated areas using precision technologies to promote more efficient water use, reducing agricultural water demand.
primary_causal_pathway: Same agricultural production service → ↓ irrigation water input/withdrawals → ↓ water stress and resource demand
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Farmers/agencies deploy precision irrigation (e.g., drip/micro-irrigation, soil-moisture/ET sensors, variable-rate scheduling, smart controllers) and related infrastructure management. (2) Mediating process: More accurate water delivery and timing reduces conveyance losses, evaporation, runoff, and deep percolation, increasing application efficiency. (3) Outcome: Lower agricultural water demand/withdrawals (and often improved drought robustness); potential secondary outcomes can include reduced energy for pumping/pressurization and associated GHG reductions, but these are not the primary effect for all cases.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing agricultural freshwater withdrawals through more efficient irrigation (delivering the same crop service with less water input).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: precision irrigation adoption primarily reduces the water input required to provide agricultural production (efficiency/resource-demand reduction).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be mitigation co-benefits (less pumping energy) and adaptation benefits (greater resilience to drought), the mechanism as stated is explicitly about “more efficient water use” and “reducing agricultural water demand,” which aligns most directly with Resource Efficiency.  
3) Edge cases applied: If policies explicitly focus on reducing energy/GHG from irrigation pumping, the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; if framed primarily as reducing drought vulnerability/exposure, it could shift toward Adaptation. Those shifts require policy-level evidence beyond the mechanism label.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that is truly “precision irrigation technology adoption → agricultural water use reduction” has its core effect as reduced freshwater withdrawals per unit of agricultural service, which is Resource Efficiency.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., mitigation co-benefits, drought-resilience outcomes) is deferred to policy-level evidence and should not change the primary category unless it becomes the dominant stated/expected impact.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/expansion effects: improved efficiency can enable expansion of irrigated area or increased water use (“efficiency paradox”) unless paired with caps/allocations; such cases may weaken actual withdrawal reductions. Energy/GHG impacts vary (pressurized drip can increase energy; reduced pumping depth can decrease it). If the policy’s main aim is drought adaptation rather than efficiency, classification could change at policy level.
key_indicators: “Irrigated infrastructure area with precision technologies”; “promote efficient water use”; “10%”; “50%”
co_benefits: Potential reduced energy use for pumping/pressurization and associated GHG reductions; possible yield stability under drought.
instance_edge_case_notes: Expressed as an adoption/coverage target (share of irrigated area with precision tech), not a quantified water-withdrawal performance standard; no explicit enforcement or funding mechanism stated.
